critique.summary: The article claims that Denmark, along with France, Germany, and Nordic allies, prepared for a potential full-scale war with the United States over Greenland in January 2026. This was reportedly in response to perceived threats from then-President Donald Trump, including his previous interest in acquiring Greenland and a US military operation in Venezuela.
mainPoints: Denmark allegedly prepared to resist a US invasion of Greenland with military support from France, Germany, and Nordic countries.
Preparations included sending elite troops, F-35 jets with live ammunition, and plans to destroy runways to prevent US military landings.
These actions were reportedly prompted by Trump's threats to seize Greenland and a US attack on Venezuela in January 2026.
A northern European coalition was formed under the guise of a defense exercise to bolster Greenland's defenses.
France, Sweden, Norway, the UK, and Germany reportedly contributed military support to deter US aggression.
critique.truthfulness: The claims in the article are based primarily on a report from the Danish public broadcaster DR, which is a credible source. However, the events described—such as a US attack on Venezuela in 2026 and the involvement of Donald Trump as US president at that time—are not supported by any verifiable information as of the current knowledge cutoff in June 2024. Donald Trump was not president in 2026, and no credible sources confirm a US military operation in Venezuela or preparations for war over Greenland. The story appears to be speculative or fictional, possibly a hypothetical or satirical scenario rather than factual reporting.
considerations: The article does not provide direct links to official statements or multiple independent sources confirming these events.
The timeline and political context described contradict known facts about US presidencies and international events up to mid-2024.
The geopolitical implications of such a conflict would likely be widely reported by multiple international news agencies, which is not the case.
The article relies heavily on anonymous sources and social media posts, which reduces its credibility.
The mention of a US attack on Venezuela and Trump’s presidency in 2026 is inconsistent with publicly available information.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Denmark's reported preparations for a potential conflict with the US over Greenland, with commenters debating the credibility of the reports, the geopolitical implications, and the broader context of US-European relations under recent US administrations. Some emphasize the routine nature of military preparedness, while others express concern over deteriorating US alliances and the plausibility of such a conflict.

comments.author: bryanlarsen
comments.text:
Keep in mind that militaries are always preparing for war. They have to. A military exists in large part to always be prepared for the unthinkable.

And in the case of countries like Denmark who have few realistic enemy choices, that means they must be prepared for unrealistic invasions, even if the US isn't threatening to invade.

Yes the Danes probably spend most of their time preparing to fight the Russians, but always wargaming the same thing leaves them unprepared for different enemies or unexpected approaches from expected enemies.

Yes, the actions in the links are more than just wargaming, but a large part of it is stuff the military should be doing anyways.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic and measured perspective on military preparedness, emphasizing that planning for unlikely scenarios is standard practice and not necessarily indicative of imminent conflict.

comments.author: the_af
comments.text:
I have no doubts they took US threats seriously; anyone who doesn't these days is a fool (of course, this doesn't mean Trump will do anything he claims, but he's dangerous enough you can never tell for sure).

What I find harder to believe is that they were preparing for "full-scale war". That makes no sense. Using F-35, American made and very likely with kill switches or otherwise susceptible to American interference? And where would they get their American made parts and supplies? And Denmark stands no chance at all against US military might, with or without assistance from France.

I'm sure they were prepared to engage in token resistance, and also more serious diplomatic and economic struggles, but "full-scale war" is hyperbole.
comments.analysis: The commenter critically assesses the claim of preparing for full-scale war, highlighting practical limitations and suggesting the response was more symbolic or diplomatic rather than an actual war plan.

comments.author: smcin
comments.text:
Source cited by @chriso-wiki.bsky.social is this article on DR.dk, the Danish public broadcaster:

https://www.dr.dk/nyheder/indland/groenland/danmark-forbered...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a credible source for the claim, helping ground the discussion in verifiable information and encouraging fact-checking.

comments.author: silvestrov
comments.text:
> * The Danish public broadcaster DR reports ...*

This is the source article (in Danish) for the bluesky posts:

https://www.dr.dk/nyheder/indland/groenland/danmark-forbered...
comments.analysis: Similar to the previous comment, it points to the original source, aiding transparency and verification.

comments.author: jeroenhd
comments.text:
Every week, the USA finds a new way to lose credibility as a serious nation. If it weren't for the observably fair elections, you'd almost think America was being taken over from the inside by foreign infiltrators.

It's ludicrous to see the USA threaten to invade a well-connected European country, invade a South American country weeks after, and then now, three months later, beg its European allies to help with the invasion of Iran because ostensibly American leadership couldn't foresee that war in the Middle East might impact fuel prices. I still think it's a ruse to distract the European military by sending the navy to the Middle East but who knows with the current idiot in charge.

I hope the country will recover some normalcy in post-Trump decade(s), but I fear we're witnessing the slow collapse of a world power. Regardless of anyone's feelings on grip the East/West dichotomy has had over the world in the past 90 or so years, such shifts in world power rarely go calmly and peacefully.
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, this comment reflects a widely discussed geopolitical view regarding US global standing and internal challenges, providing context to the tensions described.

comments.author: jacquesm
comments.text:
Of course they were. The United States has never before damaged its own reputation in Europe as much as they did in the last 12 months.

And the same goes for Canada, possibly worse. You don't go around threatening your allies unless you really have plans and that's why you don't elect senile old guys to positions of power.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a critical but plausible viewpoint on recent US-European relations, though it is somewhat generalized and lacks specific evidence.

comments.author: krona
comments.text:
I live very close to one of the USAF's largest European airbases.

While Trump was trolling European leaders about their security posture (by threatening to relieve them of sovereign territory which the US already has extensive access to) the USAF was already moving assets in the opposite direction to the middle east (this was mid-january).

It's fairly easy to work out what's happening if you ignore the orange man and listen to what serious people are saying, what they've briefed on, how they contradict one another, and where the assets are moving.

Obviously European leaders have to pretend to take the orange man seriously, but the reaction in the media was bordering on hysterical.
comments.analysis: Provides an informed perspective based on observed military movements, suggesting that rhetoric and actual military strategy may differ.

comments.author: emmelaich
comments.text: The idea that the US would attempt to take Greenland by force is utterly ludicrous.
comments.analysis: This comment dismisses the plausibility of a US invasion, reflecting a common skepticism about the practicality of such an event.

comments.author: crispyambulance
comments.text:
There's a difference between "posturing" for show and actually "preparing for war".

They're wise to the fact that "the Stable Genius" isn't going to try anything violent with Denmark/Greenland, but they still want to prevent him thinking about just stealing territory "peacefully."
comments.analysis: Highlights the nuance between military signaling and genuine war preparations, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: vrganj
comments.text:
> As a source puts it, the French said: "Would you like more soldiers? You could have them. Would you like more naval support? You could have that. Would you like more air support? You could have that too."

Thank God for the French. I long thought their strong Gaullist stance on sovereignty was a bit silly in today's world, but turns out they were right along.

Europe can't trust any outside powers. Any external dependency can and will be used against us. We used to be wide-eyed believers in international corporation and global alliances, but those are, as it turns out, always a risk and a liability.

I sure as hell am glad the French kept being stubborn enough to build most capabilities in-house, so now we have our own nuclear deterrent, aircraft carrier and fighter jet programs. Imagine if we had gone all-in on American weapons tech! They'd have us, excuse my French, by the balls!
comments.analysis: Offers a broader geopolitical reflection on European sovereignty and reliance on external powers, which is relevant but somewhat tangential.
